mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 / XFR engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned XF 5.0 V8 (AJ133) engine typically costs £4,000–£7,500 supply only for the naturally aspirated version, while the supercharged XFR engine costs £5,500–£10,000. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 / XFR engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ133 is a glorious engine but has a mixed reputation. Early cars (2009–2011) suffered from timing chain guide wear — Jaguar revised the chains, sprockets, guides and tensioners in 2012. All AJ133s suffer from plastic coolant pipe failures (the most common issue), water pump and thermostat housing failures, and HPFP issues. The XFR adds supercharger coupler wear. Regular 5,000–6,000 mile oil changes are absolutely essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 / XFR?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain guide wear (pre-2012 cars are the highest risk), plastic coolant pipe and crossover pipe failure (the defining weak point of the AJ133), supercharger coupler wear (XFR), high-pressure fuel pump failure, water pump and thermostat housing failure, and injector/PCV issues. See the Era Map above for generation-specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 / XFR engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept XFR (values £8,000–£25,000+ depending on condition and mileage), a £5,500–£7,000 reconditioned engine is 25–70% of the vehicle's value — often worth it for a solid example. The Jaguar scrap threshold of 60% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing. Given the XFR's status as a future classic and rising values, engine replacement is often worth it for a good example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 / XFR engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code (AJ133 or AJ133S) is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The naturally aspirated 5.0 V8 (385 PS) uses the AJ133, while the supercharged XFR (510 PS) and XFR-S (550 PS) use the AJ133S. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the AJ133 timing chain, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain, guide, tensioner and sprocket replacement costs £2,000–£4,000. However, if the chain snaps, a full engine replacement (£5,500–£10,000) is usually required. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. If you own a pre-2012 XFR, the timing chain issue is a known weakness that should be addressed proactively.

eraMap.title: The XF 5.0 V8 / XFR THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: JLR's clean-sheet V8 — 385 PS, 515 Nm; timing chain guides and plastic coolant pipes are the defining weak points
Early XFR — 510 PS, 625 Nm; same timing chain and coolant issues, plus supercharger coupler wear
Revised XFR with 8-speed auto — 510 PS; XFR-S 550 PS; timing chain revisions from 2012 improve durability

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 / XFR Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the XF 5.0 V8 / XFR AJ133 era (2009–2015). Supercharged XFR engines command higher prices than naturally aspirated units. A timing chain replacement alone can cost £2,000–£4,000, while a full engine rebuild can exceed £10,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an XF 5.0 V8 / XFR engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The V8's size and supercharger (on XFR) add significant complexity. Replacing head gaskets is a 25-hour labour job exclusive of machine work.

cta.label: → Compare XF 5.0 V8 / XFR engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Guide & Tensioner Failure (AJ133 — Early 2009–2011)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic clattering, engine warning light, loss of power, potential engine failure if chain skips or snaps.
problems.repairCost: £2,000–£4,000 (timing chain, guides, tensioners and sprockets replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£10,000 (if chain snaps and valves meet pistons)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The AJ133's timing chain system is the engine's Achilles' heel. Early 5.0L V8s (2009–2011) used magnesium alloy guides that were softer than the steel tensioners, causing the tensioners to wear into the guides and eventually run out of adjustment. Jaguar revised the chains, sprockets, guides and tensioners in 2012 and changed the oil spec to improve tensioner pressure. A cold-start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston-valve contact and a full engine replacement. Frequent oil changes (every 5,000–6,000 miles) are essential to prevent timing chain issues.

problems.title: Plastic Coolant Pipe & Crossover Pipe Failure (All AJ133)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, sweet smell from engine bay, low coolant warning, overheating, coolant pooling under the supercharger.
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,500 (coolant pipe replacement — supercharger removal required on XFR)【PLACEHOLDER】
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice coolant loss or overheating, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: The most unreliable part of the AJ133 is the engine coolant system, specifically the plastic pipes, hoses and tubes — particularly the two cross-over pipes at the front of the engine and the heater pipe at the rear. On supercharged cars, the extra heat from the supercharger accelerates degradation of the plastic pipes. The original plastic pipes were notorious for splitting along welded seams — upgraded seamless designs were introduced around 2016. If your XFR hasn't had these pipes replaced with the updated design, consider it essential preventative maintenance. Supercharger removal is required for access, making this a labour-intensive job.

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler Wear (XFR — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Loud rattling noise from the top of the engine on cold start, like marbles in a tin can; rattle is irregular and non-rhythmical, fading in and out; loss of supercharger efficiency.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can lead to supercharger damage

problems.recommendation: The supercharger pulley coupler is a weak design by JLR. The factory OEM spring coupler is prone to fatigue and failure. The classic symptom of a badly worn coupler is a loud rattle from the top of the engine at cold start up. To check, disconnect the drive belt and rock the pulley forwards and back to check for play. Replacement couplers are available relatively cheaply, but fitting requires supercharger removal. At the first sign of rattling from the supercharger area, have the coupler inspected.

problems.title: High-Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure (AJ133)
problems.symptoms: Hard starting, hesitation on acceleration, misfires, loss of power under load, engine warning light, P0087 code (fuel rail/system pressure too low).
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£10,000 (if fuel system failure leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if hesitation or hard starting occurs

problems.recommendation: The AJ133's high-pressure fuel pump is driven by the camshaft and works in tandem with a low-pressure fuel pump. Over time, wear, contamination, or internal failure can lead to drivability issues. Symptoms include hard starting, hesitation, misfires, or loss of power under load. If you experience any of these, have the HPFP inspected immediately. Replacing the pump before it fails completely can prevent damage to other fuel system components.

problems.title: Water Pump & Thermostat Housing Failure (AJ133)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, sweet smell from engine bay, low coolant warning, temperature fluctuations.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant loss or overheating occurs

problems.recommendation: The AJ133's plastic thermostat housing is prone to cracking. The water pump can also fail. On supercharged cars, the oil cooler-to-water pump pipe is another known failure point. If you notice coolant loss or temperature fluctuations, have the cooling system pressure-tested immediately. Proactive replacement of the thermostat housing and water pump at 60,000 miles is recommended preventative maintenance.

problems.title: Injector & PCV Valve Issues (AJ133)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, misfires, poor fuel economy, engine warning light, split PCV diaphragms.
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£10,000 (if injector failure leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can affect drivability

problems.recommendation: The AJ133's fuel injectors can fail, with some owners reporting injectors stuck so tight that a chain needed to be wrapped around them for removal. PCV diaphragms can also split, causing running issues. If you experience rough idle or misfires, have the injectors and PCV system inspected.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 / XFR — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — far cheaper than engine replacement; 2012+ cars are lower risk
Labour-intensive but essential; upgraded seamless pipes are a permanent fix
Coupler replacement is cost-effective; catch it before supercharger damage
Catch it before fuel system damage occurs
Straightforward replacement; catch it before overheating
Replacement is straightforward; catch it before engine damage
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This XF 5.0 V8 / XFR? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes at 5,000–6,000 mile intervals (not JLR's extended schedule)
* 2012+ model year (timing chain revisions applied from factory)
* Timing chain work already completed (especially on pre-2012 cars)
* Upgraded seamless coolant pipes fitted (factory pipe failure is common)
* XFR: supercharger coupler inspected and no cold-start rattle
* No coolant loss or overheating history
avoidIf: * No proof of regular oil changes — the AJ133 is unforgiving of neglect
* Pre-2012 with cold-start rattle — timing chain work is imminent and expensive
* Any coolant loss with no diagnosis — plastic coolant pipes may be failing
* XFR with loud rattling on cold start — supercharger coupler may be worn
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — the AJ133 can hide expensive faults
* "Cheap" examples — the purchase price is the least of your worries

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 and XFR represent the pinnacle of Jaguar's performance saloon range — a glorious supercharged V8 with 510–550 PS, a soundtrack to die for, and a chassis that rivals the best in the segment. The AJ133 is a clean-sheet JLR design with genuine character, but it demands respect: timing chain guide wear, plastic coolant pipe failure, supercharger coupler wear, and HPFP issues are the key weak points. Buy on service history, prioritise a 2012+ car with the revised timing system, and budget for preventative coolant pipe replacement. The XFR is a future classic — one owner described it as "a wonderful car to drive, much loved" but requiring vigilance. Neglect it, and the £30 part that fails can cost you an engine.

cta.label: → Compare XF 5.0 V8 / XFR engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar XF 5.0 V8 or XFR (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle from the front of the engine (timing chain guide warning) or top of the engine (supercharger coupler on XFR)
* Service History — Oil changes at 5,000–6,000 mile intervals are essential for the AJ133
* Model Year — 2012+ cars have revised timing chains, guides and tensioners
* Coolant Level — Check cold and hot; any loss indicates plastic coolant pipe issues
* Engine Temperature — Any fluctuations? Water pump or thermostat may be failing
* Supercharger Noise (XFR) — Any rattling on cold start? Coupler wear is common
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Timing, coolant, HPFP, or injector codes are most common
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures (often related to fuel system or injector issues)
